caption.text: Clarendon Hill in Somerville reflects a broader transformation typical of the city itself: what was once a landscape shaped by transportation and industry—rail lines, nearby freight corridors, and even earlier quarrying and material extraction—gradually evolved into a residential and community-centered space. In the 19th century, the arrival of railroads and industrial infrastructure across Somerville fueled rapid growth, drawing workers and industries to areas like Clarendon Hill’s surroundings.
caption.title: Mystic River (Clarendon Hill)

caption.text: In the 19th and early 20th centuries, the area surrounding Clarendon Hill was deeply influenced by the rise of factories and industrial development across Somerville. Its proximity to expanding rail lines made it an ideal location for industry, as manufacturers relied on efficient transportation to move raw materials and finished goods. Brickmaking, meatpacking, and other forms of light manufacturing grew rapidly nearby, supported by access to regional markets in Boston. These industries drew large numbers of immigrant and working-class residents, leading to dense housing development in places like Clarendon Hill. Over time, as industrial activity declined in the mid-20th century, many of these factory sites were abandoned, repurposed, or replaced, paving the way for the neighborhood’s gradual shift toward residential use and community-centered redevelopment.
caption.title: The Beginning of Industrial Development; Tuft's Brick Manufacturing Company

caption.text: In the late 1800s, the Mystic River and its reservoir system were central to the environmental and infrastructural landscape surrounding Tufts University, then known as Tufts College. Located just below the hilltop campus, the Mystic River basin provided both a practical water source and a defining geographic feature for the area. As the region industrialized, the river and its managed reservoirs supported nearby factories, brickworks, and growing residential neighborhoods by supplying water and aiding in drainage. Its proximity to Tufts meant that the college developed within a landscape shaped by both natural resources and engineered systems, where access to water was essential for sustaining daily operations and the surrounding community. At the same time, the presence of the Mystic River highlighted the tension between industrial growth and environmental conditions in the late 19th century. While it enabled expansion and supported infrastructure like pumping stations, the river was also impacted by pollution from nearby industries and urban runoff. For Tufts College, this meant existing alongside a working industrial environment, rather than the more suburban academic setting it might suggest today. Over time, as industrial activity declined and environmental awareness increased, the river shifted from a utilitarian resource to a valued natural and recreational space, mirroring the broader transformation of the area from industry to community-oriented development.
caption.title: Developing Infrastructure; Mytsic Reservoir

caption.text: This image from Digital Commonwealth captures the Mystic River in its late 19th-century, working landscape—visually reinforcing how closely water infrastructure, industry, and nearby institutions like Tufts University were intertwined. The presence of managed water, surrounding development, and built structures suggests a river actively shaped for utility, not just scenery, highlighting its role in supporting both industrial activity and the growth of the Tufts campus.
caption.title: Mystic Reservoir 1898

caption.text: Pumping stations played a quieter but essential role in shaping the industrial and residential landscape around Clarendon Hill, linking infrastructure to both public health and urban growth. As nearby factories, railways, and dense working-class housing expanded across Somerville, the need for reliable water supply and effective sewage removal became critical. Pumping stations helped move water to higher elevations like Clarendon Hill and carried waste away from increasingly crowded neighborhoods, making large-scale industrial production and residential life possible. Without this infrastructure, the area’s growth—fueled by brick manufacturing, rail access, and proximity to Boston—would have been far more limited and far less sanitary.
caption.title: Mystic Waterworks

caption.text: The Mystic Waterworks, tied to the Mystic River system, remained a constant and stabilizing presence in the infrastructure surrounding Clarendon Hill from the late 19th into the early 20th century. As Somerville continued to industrialize and expand, this network of reservoirs, dams, and pumping stations ensured a steady supply of water for factories, rail operations, and growing residential neighborhoods. Even as the area began shifting away from heavy industry, the Mystic Waterworks persisted as an essential backbone of daily life, supporting the transition toward a more permanent, community-oriented environment.
caption.title: Mystic Waterworks 1895

caption.text: Today, the Mystic Water Works no longer functions as an active part of the region’s water supply, but it remains an important historical and architectural landmark. Originally built in the 1860s as part of the Mystic River water system, the facility was eventually decommissioned in the early 20th century as Boston’s water infrastructure shifted to larger, more centralized systems.  In recent years, the building has been repurposed rather than abandoned—most notably converted into affordable senior housing while preserving its historic exterior and key structural elements.  This adaptive reuse reflects a broader trend in Somerville, where former industrial infrastructure is integrated into contemporary community life, allowing the Mystic Water Works to remain a visible and meaningful part of the area’s landscape even as its original function has disappeared.
caption.title: Mystic Waterworks Today

caption.text: Railway transportation in the Clarendon Hill area functioned in close coordination with both industrial development and waterworks infrastructure, forming an interconnected system that sustained growth in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Rail lines moving through Somerville provided factories with efficient access to raw materials and regional markets, while also relying on nearby water systems—like those connected to the Mystic River and Mystic Water Works—for steam power, processing, and sanitation. At the same time, water infrastructure depended on rail networks to transport construction materials and maintain large-scale systems like reservoirs and pumping stations. Together, these systems created a mutually reinforcing network: railways enabled industrial expansion, factories increased demand for reliable water, and waterworks made dense industrial and residential life sustainable. This overlap is especially visible in areas near Tufts University, where transportation corridors, industrial sites, and engineered water systems existed side by side. Over time, even as factories declined, the transportation infrastructure remained, eventually shifting from moving goods to moving people—carrying forward the legacy of connectivity that first defined the area’s industrial landscape.
caption.title: Railway Transportation

caption.text: The West End Street Railway played a major role in shaping the development of neighborhoods like Clarendon Hill by transforming how people moved through and between cities in the late 19th century. As one of the first companies to consolidate and electrify streetcar lines in the Boston area, it made travel more reliable and accessible for working-class residents. This expanded transit network connected places like Somerville to Boston, allowing workers to live farther from industrial job sites while still maintaining daily access to them. In this way, the street railway helped shift the area from being strictly tied to rail yards and factories toward a more flexible, residential landscape.
caption.title: West End Street Railway Co.

caption.text: What remains as an important part of the city’s transportation services are railway stations that continue to provide connection and outreach across the region. In Somerville, stations along the MBTA network—particularly those on the Green Line Extension—represent a modern continuation of the area’s long history of mobility and access. These stations not only link residents to Boston and surrounding communities, but also serve as anchors for neighborhood development, encouraging walkability, local business growth, and greater accessibility to jobs and education. In places like Clarendon Hill, where earlier rail infrastructure once supported industry and freight, today’s stations instead prioritize the movement of people, reinforcing a shift toward a more connected, community-oriented urban environment.
caption.title: Somerville Station

caption.text: Clarendon Hill’s early development was shaped in large part by the expansion of rail infrastructure in the greater Boston area, particularly lines associated with what is now the MBTA system. In the 19th and early 20th centuries, nearby rail corridors connected Somerville to Boston and surrounding industrial centers, making areas like Clarendon Hill attractive for working-class housing due to their accessibility to jobs in factories, shipyards, and freight depots. These railways facilitated not just the movement of goods, but also daily life—bringing workers in and out of the city and linking neighborhoods to a broader economic network. Over time, as industrial reliance on rail declined and transportation shifted toward roads and public transit, the prominence of these rail lines diminished, allowing spaces like Clarendon Hill to transition away from industrial dependence and toward more residential, community-focused development.
caption.title: 1892 map of West End's system (bolded), a few years after consolidation
